rationale: The citing paper explicitly positions itself against prior health messaging research centered on framing, and this cited paper is a core health-framing study on mammography persuasion with affective mechanisms (affect × gain/loss frame effects on persuasion, risk, anxiety). Given the strong construct and domain overlap (health persuasion, detection context, affect/fear-related processes) and a confirmed DOI match, this is plausibly a theory/mechanism source rather than an incidental mention.
